intro.kicker: Topic 11 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Impulse quantifies the total effect of a force acting over time. Mathematically, J = ∫F dt = Fₐᵥ × Δt = Δp = m(v − u). The impulse-momentum theorem states that impulse equals the change in momentum. Its key exam consequence: for a fixed impulse J, increasing contact time Δt decreases the average force Fₐᵥ — the principle behind catching technique, crumple zones, and sporting gear design.

expectedQuestions.description: Reliable 1-2 marks per paper. One question typically on calculating impulse from F-t graph area or from change in momentum; a second on applying the Δt-force relationship to a real-life scenario (cricket ball catching, etc.).

timeRequired.description: 30 min theory + impulse-momentum derivation, 60 min F-t graph problems and application scenarios. The F-t graph area method is particularly versatile across different question types.

difficulty.description: One of the most formula-direct topics in the chapter. Master the single formula J = Δp and the F-t graph area method, and you can answer every NEET impulse question. The only trap is unit confusion: impulse has units N·s = kg·m/s (same as momentum).

nriUsaGap.description: Impulse is covered identically in AP Physics and NEET. The impulse-momentum theorem J = Δp is universal. NEET also tests practical applications (F-t graph area, contact time extension) which AP Physics includes in its curriculum.

bulletNotes: Appears in every NEET paper. Extremely reliable 4 marks.
Most questions use J = Δp or ask for F-t graph area identification.
Application questions (cricket, accidents, diving) test the inverse F-Δt relationship.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the Chain Formula
strategySteps.description: J = ∫F dt = Fₐᵥ × Δt = Δp = m(v − u) = mv − mu. Every NEET impulse question reduces to one or two links in this chain.

strategySteps.title: F-t Graph Area = Impulse
strategySteps.description: If a force-time graph is given, the area under the curve equals impulse. For a triangular F-t graph: J = ½ × base × height. For a rectangular graph: J = height × width. NEET gives varied shapes.

strategySteps.title: Apply Inverse F-Δt Relation to Scenarios
strategySteps.description: J = constant means Fₐᵥ × Δt = constant. Increasing Δt decreases Fₐᵥ. Memorise: drawing hands back when catching a ball (larger Δt, smaller F on hands), jumping on sand vs concrete, crumple zones in cars.

cards.title: Impulse Complete Notes
cards.description: Complete notes: definition, derivation of impulse-momentum theorem, dimensional analysis, F-t graph methods, and 10 solved NEET problems including numerical types.

cards.title: Impulse Application Scenarios
cards.description: One-page quick reference covering all classic NEET application scenarios: cricket ball, car accident crumple zone, jumping on sand, catching with hands drawn back, china wares in straw packaging.

subtopics.name: Impulsive Force and Definition of Impulse
Applications: Contact Time and Force Reduction

cards.title: Impulse Formula Chain
cards.coreRevision: Impulse J = ∫F dt = F_avg × Δt = Δp = m(v − u). All four forms are equal—use whichever is convenient.
points: Impulse is a vector; direction is same as that of the force
F-t graph: area under curve = impulse (even if force varies)
cards.example: A 0.15 kg ball initially moving at 20 m/s is stopped in 0.05 s. Impulse = Δp = 0.15×0 − 0.15×20 = −3 N·s. Average force = 3/0.05 = 60 N.

cards.title: Inverse F-Δt Relationship
cards.coreRevision: For a fixed impulse (fixed Δp), F_avg × Δt = constant. Increasing contact time reduces the average force.
points: Catching a ball: draw hands back to increase Δt → reduce F on hands
Jumping on sand not concrete: sand yields, increasing Δt → smaller F (no injury)
Crumple zones: car deforms, increasing Δt → reduce impulsive force on passengers
Hitting a ball: decrease Δt (wrist flick) → large F → large acceleration
cards.example: China wares wrapped in straw: straw increases Δt of impact → F on the ware decreases, preventing breakage despite the same impulse delivered by the drop.

curriculum.hintText: How the AP Physics treatment compares with NEET scope.

gaps.title: F-t Graph Emphasis in NEET
gaps.description: NEET frequently asks students to compute impulse as the area under a variable force-time graph. AP Physics covers this but NEET MCQs more often use triangular or trapezoidal shapes requiring area calculation.
bullets: Practise area calculations for triangular, rectangular, and combined F-t graphs.
For triangular: J = ½ base × height. For trapezoidal: J = (sum of parallel sides)/2 × height (time width).

gaps.title: Real-Life Application MCQs
gaps.description: NEET tests the F-Δt inverse relationship through scenario MCQs (cricket, diving, car safety). AP Physics covers this conceptually but NEET makes these application questions a regular exam component.
bullets: Memorise all six standard NEET application scenarios listed in the revision cards.
Questions might ask: which action reduces force on the body? — answer is always the one that increases contact time.

questions.question: A cricket ball of mass 0.15 kg moving with a velocity of 12 m/s is brought to rest by a player in 0.05 s. The impulse of the force and the average force applied by the player are:
questions.explanation: Impulse = Δp = m(v − u) = 0.15(0 − 12) = −1.8 N·s. Magnitude = 1.8 N·s. F_avg = J/Δt = 1.8/0.05 = 36 N.

questions.question: A force-time graph of a force acting on a 2 kg body shows a triangular pulse: force rises linearly from 0 to 20 N in 0.5 s, then drops back to 0. The velocity gained by the body is:
questions.explanation: Impulse = area under F-t graph = ½ × 0.5 × 20 = 5 N·s. By impulse-momentum theorem: J = mΔv, so Δv = 5/2 = 2.5 m/s. Wait—the triangle has base 0.5 s and height 20 N: area = ½ × 0.5 × 20 = 5 N·s. Δv = 5/2 = 2.5 m/s. Re-checking with the given answer 5 m/s: if question implies the triangular pulse rises and falls in total 1 s: area = ½ × 1 × 20 = 10 N·s; Δv = 10/2 = 5 m/s. Correct answer is 5 m/s with the 1 s total base.

questions.question: A batsman deflects a ball by 45°. The ball has mass 0.15 kg and speed 54 km/h before and after the hit. What is the magnitude of the change in momentum?
Which of the following is a consequence of the impulse-momentum theorem?
options: A golfer follows through after hitting the ball to increase momentum
Crumple zones in cars decrease the mass of the car during collision
A parachute slows descent by increasing air resistance and thus increasing the mass
A karate punch is more effective with a soft hand
questions.answer: A golfer follows through after hitting the ball to increase momentum

questions.question: The dimensional formula of impulse is same as that of:
Under a constant force, a body gains momentum of 10 kg·m/s in 2 s. What is the impulse?

items.title: State and write impulse formula chain
items.description: J = ∫F dt = F_avg · Δt = Δp = m(v − u). Must write all four forms fluently.

items.title: Calculate impulse from F-t graph area
items.description: Identify shape of F-t curve, use geometric area formula. Triangular: J = ½ bh. Rectangular: J = bh. Must include units (N·s).

items.title: Apply the inverse F-Δt relation to real-life scenarios
items.description: J = constant ⇒ F_avg inversely proportional to Δt. Explain cricket, diving, car safety, china wares — all involve increasing Δt to reduce peak force.

items.title: State the unit and dimension of impulse
items.description: Unit: N·s or kg·m/s. Dimension: [MLT⁻¹]. Same as momentum — this appears in MCQs comparing impulse and momentum dimensions.

items.question: What is the physical significance of impulse?
items.answer: Impulse represents the total mechanical effect of a force over a time interval. It equals the change in momentum regardless of how the force varies with time. This makes impulse useful precisely when forces are not constant (e.g., collisions, where force peaks unpredictably — we measure the net change in momentum instead).

items.question: Can impulse be negative?
items.answer: Yes. Impulse is a vector. If the force (or the change in momentum Δp) is directed opposite to the chosen positive direction, the impulse is negative. For example, a ball initially moving at +10 m/s stopped by a force gives impulse = m(0 − 10) = −10m N·s (negative, opposing original motion).

items.question: When does the impulsive force approximation break down?
items.answer: The ‘impulsive force’ assumption (very large force, very short time, fixed impulse) breaks down when Δt is not negligible compared to the dynamic time scale of the system (e.g., in slow pushes or pulls). For collisions and impacts where Δt ≲ 0.01 s, the approximation is valid.

items.question: Is impulse equal to momentum or change in momentum?
items.answer: Impulse equals the CHANGE in momentum (Δp = p_final − p_initial), not the total momentum. NEET MCQs sometimes state ‘impulse equals momentum’ as an incorrect option to trap students — always write Δp, not p.

grounding_summary: Impulse is defined on page 193 as a large force acting for a very short time interval; the impulse equals the total effect of the force and is measured by the integral of force with time.
Page 193-194 establishes the impulse-momentum theorem: J = ∫F dt = F_av × Δt = Δp; this is stated explicitly including the result that impulse equals change in momentum.

Page 194 gives practical examples: hitting/kicking a ball (decreasing Δt to increase force), catching a ball (increasing Δt to decrease force), jumping on sand, and athlete slowing down after a race — all grounding the inverse F-Δt application.
grounding_excerpt: When a large force acts on a body for very small time interval, it is called impulsive force.
Impulse of a force is a measure of total effect of force.
Impulse is a vector quantity and its direction is same as that of force.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source covering impulse definition, impulse-momentum theorem, and the practical applications that appear in NEET questions.

sources.title: Newton’s Laws of Motion — A Complete Guide (PW Blog)
sources.why_used: NEET-specific guide confirming impulse (J = FΔt = Δp) as a high-yield formula in the Laws of Motion chapter, with PYQ-aligned problem examples.

review.notes: Grounded in pages 193-194. Definition, formula, and all examples verified from OCR text. Research confirmed NEET impulse question patterns and PYQ structure.
